data.title: Ameren: A Good Safe Haven Play, But Valuation Looks Questionable
data.description:
Ameren Corporation (AEE) is a regulated electric and natural gas utility that primarily serves residential and business customers who are located in the U.S. states of Missouri and Illinois. Ameren is a relatively large utility, especially when compared to some of the other utility companies that we have discussed over the past several months. This comes, at least partially, from the fact that Ameren serves some of the largest cities in each of the two states. For example, Ameren serves the city of St. Louis, Missouri, which is the second-largest city in Missouri. More importantly, however, Ameren possesses many of the characteristics that have made utility companies some of the most common holdings among conservative, risk-averse investors. The company also managed to achieve significant growth over the course of 2025, which is also something that many investors will appreciate. Finally, Ameren has a very reasonable valuation at the current price. These factors combined might make it a decent holding for anyone who is looking to ride through the current market volatility.

About Ameren Corporation

As mentioned in the introduction, Ameren Corporation is a regulated electric and natural gas utility that serves customers in the U.S. states of Missouri and Illinois. The company's service territory is quite large, as it includes most of the Greater St. Louis, Missouri, area, as well as most of Illinois, other than the city of Chicago:

While significant portions of both Missouri and Illinois are rural, with Illinois alone having 75% of its total land area dedicated to farms, there are still quite a lot of individuals living within Ameren's service territory. In fact, Ameren has approximately 2.5 million electric customers and 900,000 natural gas utility customers as of December 31, 2025. These customers are almost evenly split between Missouri and Illinois, particularly in the electric space:

State

Electric Customers

Natural Gas Customers

Missouri

1.3 million

100,000

Illinois

1.2 million

800,000

This is actually somewhat nice because it limits Ameren's exposure to a single state's regulatory regime. For example, Illinois is a so-called "deregulated" state in which multiple suppliers produce and sell electricity (although transmission and distribution are still regulated by the authorities). Missouri, on the other hand, is not a deregulated state for electricity, and customers located within Ameren's service territory have to purchase their electricity at the price approved by the Missouri state regulator.

It is somewhat questionable whether regulated or deregulated states are better for utility company profits. The original impetus of the push for deregulation back in the 1990s was that by allowing customers to choose their electricity provider, competition would be put into the markets, and this competition would push down consumer electricity prices. However, as an article published on Real Clear Energy points out:

If state lawmakers truly want to lower electric bills, pushing deregulation is a mistake. Historically, this policy has raised electric bills. In fact, customers living in deregulated states have paid an extra $19 billion dollars more for electricity.

The Real Clear Energy article cites an article that was published in the Wall Street Journal as evidence for this claim. For its part, Ameren's electric utility business in Missouri has substantially higher revenue than its business in Illinois:

State

FY 2025 Revenue

Missouri Electric Operating Revenues

$4,631

Illinois Electric Operating Revenue

$3,261

As we can see, the electric revenue that Ameren brings in from its electric utility in Missouri is substantially higher than the revenue that it brings in from its electric utility in Illinois. This is not necessarily a perfect comparison that we can actually use to compare the two markets in terms of how much the different regulatory structures affect the company's performance. This is because Ameren is a delivery-only utility in Illinois. The company does not own any generation capacity in Illinois. It merely purchases electricity from the Illinois Power Agency and delivers it to some of its customers. The company's most recent 10-K states that Ameren purchased power on behalf of 28% of its customers in Illinois during 2025. This is different from Missouri, as the company actually generates its own electricity in Missouri and sells it to its customers. This means that Ameren is not actually making any money from the electricity itself in Illinois; the company is only earning distribution and transmission fees. In Missouri, Ameren is earning the distribution and transmission fees just like in Illinois, but it also receives the generation fees from the sale of the electricity itself. Thus, it makes sense that the company would have higher revenues in Missouri, given the very similar customer counts.

One way in which regulators determine the price that a utility company, such as Ameren, is allowed to charge its customers is by allowing it to earn a specific return on equity. As Enerdynamics explains:

Return on equity, also known as ROE or the cost of equity capital, describes the return on the equity portion of the rate base that regulated utilities are allowed to collect in rates.

The article goes on to further state that regulators try to allow the utility to provide a high enough rate of return that they can actually attract investors to purchase their equity, but not so high that the people who depend on the utility for their electricity and natural gas are being overcharged. It is a balancing act, and it is certainly not an exact science, but investors should still be aware of it in order to make better decisions about which utility to purchase.

Perhaps unfortunately for investors, different states have different policies for the return on equity that a utility is allowed to earn. This is another reason why Ameren's electric utility customer base being split between Missouri and Illinois is a nice thing. The company does not have to be completely exposed to the policies of a single regulator. Ameren's allowed return on equity in Illinois varies by business unit:

Ameren Business Unit

Allowed ROE

Ameren Transmission

10.48%

Ameren Electric Distribution

8.72%

Ameren Illinois Natural Gas

9.60%

Unfortunately, the company has not been able to provide an allowed return on equity for its operations in Missouri as of the time of writing. The 10-K states that new rates became effective in June and September 2025, respectively, but the order allowing the rates did not specify an allowed return on equity:

As such, it is difficult to compare the regulatory regimes between the two states. Regardless, however, it is a pretty fair conclusion that having exposure to two states and two separate regulatory regimes provides Ameren with a diversification advantage over an electric utility that is solely exposed to a single regulatory authority.

Stability and Recent Growth

As mentioned in the introduction, Ameren has many of the characteristics that have historically made utilities reasonably popular investments among risk-averse types, as well as made them somewhat useful as a safe haven during challenging economic periods. One of these characteristics is that the company exhibits a great deal of stability regardless of macroeconomic conditions. We can see this in Ameren's revenue, which has grown from $5.909 billion in 2017 to $8.470 billion for the full-year 2025 period:

(All figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

There were a few challenging economic periods over that nine-year period. The COVID-19 pandemic and the shutdown of the economy may be the first to come to mind, as that resulted in a surge in the unemployment rate due to many businesses shutting down (either temporarily or permanently), and naturally, unemployed people frequently struggle to pay their bills. At the end of 2020, the total amount of unpaid electric and gas utility bills was approximately $32 billion, with some estimates stating that about 20% of households were behind on their utility payments at the time. As we can see in the chart above, however, Ameren was not really affected much by this, as its full-year 2020 revenue only showed a slight decline compared to 2019 levels. This alone is a testament to the company's stability in challenging macroeconomic conditions.

The pandemic of 2020 was not the only challenging period during the timeframe shown above, however. In 2022, crude oil and gasoline prices spiked along with interest rates. The American Psychological Association reports that due to increases in prices throughout the economy that year, the majority of American adults reported that financial problems were causing them stress, and 57% said that they did not have enough money to cover their basic needs. Admittedly, these results were obtained from a survey that the organization conducted, so some individuals might question the validity of these numbers, but during that same year, there were also a number of mainstream news stories discussing how rising prices throughout the economy were forcing people to make difficult decisions about skipping meals in order to pay the electric bill. As such, it seems likely that there were many people who were financially struggling during 2022. Despite this, we can see that Ameren's revenues came in much stronger in 2022 than they did in 2021. This provides further support for the idea that the company enjoys relatively stable financial performance regardless of economic conditions.

This could be a desirable characteristic for a company, given the current economic backdrop. As we all know, oil prices have surged over the past few weeks due to an outbreak of hostilities in the Middle East and the largely successful efforts of Iran to control shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz. According to the U.S. Energy Information Administration, the average price of gasoline in the United States was $2.936 per gallon in January and $3.039 per gallon in February. AAA states that the average price of gasoline in the United States was $3.93 per gallon last week. That is a fairly large increase in a product that many people use a significant amount of in a relatively short period of time. As more than half of the population of the United States claims to be living paycheck to paycheck, we can conclude that the surge in gasoline prices will cause financial difficulties for many people. Rabobank actually goes so far as to state that the surge in crude oil prices might cause a recession if high gasoline prices force enough people to cut back on consumer discretionary spending. That would obviously be a bad thing for any company that is dependent on the willingness and ability of consumers to spend. However, a company like Ameren should hold up much better since most people will prioritize paying their utility bills ahead of most discretionary purchases, and the company has a history of weathering challenging macroeconomic conditions without too much difficulty. We can see that clearly in its historical trailing revenues.

Revenue is not the only measure of financial performance that shows stability and growth for Ameren. The company's net income exhibits remarkable stability regardless of economic conditions:

(all figures in millions of U.S. dollars)

We immediately notice that there was not a single year over the entire nine-year period in which Ameren's net income declined. In each of the years, the company's reported net income came in stronger than it did in the previous year. This was even the case in 2020, with the economic shutdown of the pandemic and the surge in utility bill delinquencies still unable to cause Ameren's net income to decline. This further strengthens the case that Ameren could be a very good investment for someone who is worried about the possibility of a near-term recession.

Over the nine-year period shown, Ameren managed to grow its net income at a 12.05% compound annual growth rate. This is far greater than the rate of inflation if inflation is measured by the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers. According to that index, $523 million in January 2017 has the same buying power as $697.91 million in December 2025. Ameren's net income for the 2025 period was $1.456 billion, so obviously it managed to deliver earnings growth that was higher than can simply be explained by inflation. This is very nice to see from the perspective of an income investor. After all, as we have discussed before, one of the biggest goals of an income investor is to ensure that their money lasts for the remainder of their life. In order to do that, they need to be invested in companies and securities that manage to maintain their purchasing power over the long term. Ameren's net income appears to be doing that.

However, one of the biggest problems with utilities is that they need to keep making capital investments into their infrastructure in order to maintain their rate base. As mentioned earlier, regulators typically allow a utility company to earn a specific rate of return. The rate base is the value of the company's assets that is used for this calculation. As the value of the company's assets decreases each year due to depreciation, the utility needs to continually invest more and more money into its rate base in order to keep the value steady, let alone increase it. Thus, a utility company consistently has high capital expenditures, and it needs to raise money from investors to finance these capital expenditures. This is accomplished through the issuance of both debt and equity, the latter of which has the effect of steadily diluting the existing shareholders. As such, a utility company's earnings per share will normally rise more slowly than its net income. This is the case for Ameren, which we can see by looking at the company's earnings per share over the past nine years:

As shown, Ameren's earnings per share went from $2.16 per share in 2017 to $5.38 per share in 2025. This is only a 10.67% compound annual growth rate, or less than the company's net income growth over the same period. However, it is still more than sufficient to beat inflation. The Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers implies that $2.16 in January 2017 had the same purchasing power as $2.88 in December 2025. Obviously, Ameren Corporation managed to do quite a bit better than that. Thus, it does appear that the company has done a pretty good job of protecting investors against inflation over the nine-year period.

Naturally, as investors, we are not only interested in how well a company has done in the past, but we are also interested in what it will do in the future. Fortunately for potential investors, Ameren appears likely to continue on its historical growth trajectory. The primary way that it will do this is by growing its rate base. As we have already discussed, regulators typically allow a utility company to earn a specific return on equity, so the higher its rate base, the more dollars that specified percentage will be. Alongside its most recent earnings results, Ameren announced its capital spending plan for the 2026 to 2030 period:

Over the five-year period from 2026 to 2030, Ameren plans to invest $31.8 billion into utility infrastructure upgrades, modernization, and capacity growth. We can see that the bulk of this spending is allocated for the Missouri business as opposed to the Illinois business. This may be surprising given that the company has very similar customer counts in each state, but it becomes more understandable when we consider that Ameren is a delivery-only utility in Illinois. In Missouri, the company actually generates the electricity that it sells, and constructing generation capacity adds to the total capital expenditures needed to serve that particular state. As of right now, Ameren has around 2.6 gigawatts of new generation capacity under construction in the state of Missouri:

Energy Center

Generation Capacity

Anticipated In-Service Date

Bowling Green

50 MW

Q1 2026

Split Rail

300 MW

Q2 2026

Castle Bluff

800 MW

Q4 2027

Big Hollow BESS

400 MW

Q2 2028

Big Hollow CTG

800 MW

Q3 2028

Reform

250 MW

Q4 2028

This new capacity is scheduled to come online between now and the end of 2028, as we can see in the chart. The company's capital spending plan states that it will be spending about $14.5 billion on new generation capacity in Missouri between 2026 and 2030, which is approximately 46% of all of the company's planned capital investments over the period. The company does not, however, state whether or not the above generation plants are the only ones that it intends to construct by 2030 or if more could be in the pipeline. When we consider the need to construct generation capacity in Missouri, the fact that this state is receiving the bulk of the company's capital spending is understandable. If we subtract out the money budgeted for new generation, we see that only about $6.8 billion is being devoted to the company's non-generation infrastructure in Missouri, which is not too far off the numbers for Illinois.

As presented, Ameren's capital spending program should grow its rate base at a 10.6% compound annual growth rate through 2030:

However, it may be unrealistic to expect the company's earnings per share to grow at the same 10.6% compound annual growth rate over the period. After all, as was already mentioned, Ameren will need to raise capital to finance this growth, and part of that financing strategy will be the issuance of new shares. This will dilute the investors and prevent the company's earnings per share from growing as rapidly as either the rate base or net income. Ameren's management projects that, given the current financing needs, the company's earnings per share will only grow by about 6% to 8% over the 2026 to 2030 period. If we assume that the company's price-to-earnings ratio remains stable over the same period, then investors can likely expect the share price to increase at a similar pace. In addition to the share price appreciation, investors will also receive the dividend that the company pays out, which is currently 2.76% of the share price. This overall gives us a projected total return of 9% to 11% annually for the next five years, which is not bad at all for a utility company. However, it is also not exceptional, as most utility companies aim to deliver an average total annual return of around that level.

Recent Performance Update

The last time that we discussed Ameren Corporation was on March 16, 2025. Thus, just over a year has passed since we last looked at it, and as we all know, there are a lot of things that can occur in a year's time. This past year was no exception, as only a few weeks after my last article was published, the United States shocked the market by imposing a series of tariffs on most of its trading partners. This even led to fears that there would be a recession by the end of 2025. However, ultimately, things calmed down, and there was no recession. The market also regained all of the losses that it suffered in the aftermath of the tariff shock, and the S&P 500 Index (SP500) finished 2025 at very close to an all-time high.

The markets have been much more somber this year, however. As I mentioned in a recent article, one of the biggest reasons for this is that investors have begun to doubt whether or not generative artificial intelligence technology will truly be as profitable as they had previously estimated. The nation's largest technology companies are spending substantial amounts of money to construct data centers to support the technology, but as of yet, these same companies have not presented credible monetization strategies for the technology. The S&P 500 Index (SPY) is down 6.17% year-to-date, but that is almost entirely due to the technology companies that represent an outsized proportion of the index. The Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP), which equally weights all constituents of the index, has held up much better:

This indicates that much of the market weakness has been centered around the technology sector and, specifically, the giant technology companies that dominate the market-capitalization-weighted index.

Of course, as I mentioned earlier, the recent surge in oil prices has caused some market watchers and analysts to become concerned about a recession in the near term. Indeed, it is very possible that an extended elevation in oil prices will strain the budgets of ordinary people and cause a sufficiently large decline in consumer spending to push the economy into a recession. However, according to Moody's Analytics, about half of U.S. consumer spending comes from the wealthiest 10% of households right now, and this is the group that is least likely to be adversely affected by rising oil prices, so it is possible that rising oil prices will not have as much of an impact on consumer spending as some analysts fear. As a result, it is possible that the United States will avoid a recession, but that does not mean that families who are lower on the income and wealth scale will not have to cut back in the face of rising energy prices.

As we have already discussed, though, Ameren is a regulated business that provides a product that most people consider to be a necessity. As such, they tend to pay their utility bills even when money gets tight. This shows up in the company's finances, as they have generally proven to be quite stable regardless of the broader macroeconomic conditions. It also shows up in Ameren's stock price, which has risen by 7.93% since the last time that we discussed the company:

Unfortunately, we can see that the company's stock price underperformed the S&P 500 Index over the period, but that is not atypical, as utility companies frequently do underperform the high-growth stocks that dominate the large capitalization index.

Interestingly, Ameren has actually outperformed the S&P 500 Index significantly since the start of this year:

Ameren's share price has risen year-to-date, which is a rarity right now. After all, as anyone watching the markets can testify, most asset classes have fallen since the start of this year. The fact that Ameren's share price has actually risen helps support the thesis that this is a "safe haven" company that may be able to help investors ride out the turbulence plaguing the rest of the market.

Valuation

According to Zacks Investment Research, Ameren will grow its earnings per share at a 9.27% rate over the next three to five years. This is quite a bit higher than the company's own management projects, but it is reasonably consistent with what Ameren has delivered over the past nine years. In fact, this projection is lower than the EPS growth that Ameren has delivered since 2017. As such, it is not unreasonable, but it might be a bit optimistic.

The Zacks EPS growth rate projection gives Ameren's stock a 2.21 price-to-earnings growth ratio at the current price. Here is how that compares to some of the company's peers:

Company Name

Current PEG Ratio

Ameren Corporation

2.21

Entergy Corporation (ETR)

2.17

DTE Energy (DTE)

2.65

Exelon Corporation (EXC)

2.81

CMS Energy (CMS)

2.70

FirstEnergy Corp. (FE)

2.63

Eversource Energy (ES)

2.94

This suggests that Ameren Corporation is currently very reasonably priced compared to other utility companies. However, we should note that the above PEG ratios were calculated using the Zacks earnings projection. If instead we were to use the 7% midpoint that the company's management has projected, then Ameren's price-to-earnings growth ratio comes in at 2.92, which makes it somewhat expensive compared to its peers. As such, we might want to look at other valuation measures before making a determination about the company's valuation.

Seeking Alpha's Quant Valuation System assigns Ameren a valuation grade of "D" at the current price, noting that it is more expensive than the utility sector average on a variety of metrics:

Here is how the same system rates the company's peers:

Company Name

Seeking Alpha Quant Valuation Grade

Ameren Corporation

D

Entergy Corporation

D-

DTE Energy

C-

Exelon Corporation

C+

CMS Energy

D+

FirstEnergy Corp.

B-

Eversource Energy

B+

This system certainly suggests that Ameren is somewhat expensive at its current price. The only company shown in the chart that has a less attractive valuation is Entergy, with all of the others comparing somewhat better with the peer median overall.

Conclusion

In conclusion, Ameren has appeal as a "safe haven" investment in today's volatile market. The company has a history of weathering through challenging economic conditions with ease, and its recent stock price performance has reflected this. After all, Ameren is one of the few companies in the market that has actually delivered share price gains this year. The company provides a necessary product, and as such, its customers will likely continue to pay their bills to Ameren even if the economy worsens due to surging oil prices. The only problem with Ameren right now is that the valuation is questionable. While the stock appears to be very reasonably priced when using certain earnings projections, I am concerned that these projections are a bit overly optimistic. As such, for now, I will maintain my "hold" rating on Ameren, but this stock becomes a buy if the stock price declines to under $100 or so (which would put its forward price-to-earnings right around the utility sector median).

data.title: APOS: Back To A Buy Rating
data.description:
Introduction

The Apollo Global Management, Inc. NT 7.625% 53 (APOS) is an issue that I last covered over two years ago (article link). While the Note does not mature until 2053, Apollo has the right to Call it every five years starting in 2028. As part of my analysis, I found two other Notes with similar features as I deem that important when deciding if a single issue is a good investment choice.

My rating in 2024 for APOS was a Sell, down from a Buy due to the price jump during that time period. I currently see no risk in terms of payment or bankruptcy concerning APOS as its coverage ratios are excellent, plus backstopped by Apollo Global. With its 7.5% yield, I am again giving it a Buy rating.

Apollo Global Management Review

Seeking Alpha describes Apollo Global Management (APO) as (edited):

Apollo Global Management, Inc. is a private equity firm specializing in investments in credit, private equity, infrastructure, secondaries and real estate markets. The firm prefers to invest in private and public markets. The firm's private equity investments include traditional buyouts, recapitalization, distressed buyouts and debt investments in real estate, corporate partner buyouts, distressed asset, corporate carve-outs, middle market, growth, venture capital, turnaround, bridge, corporate restructuring, special situation, acquisition, and industry consolidation transactions. For credit strategies, the firm focuses to invest in multi-sector credit, semi-liquid credit, direct lending, first lien, unitranche, whole loans and private credit. The firm launches and manages hedge funds for its clients. It also manages real estate funds and private equity funds for its clients. The firm invests in the fixed income and alternative investment markets across the globe. The firm conducts in-house research to create its investment portfolio. Apollo Global Management, Inc. was founded in 1990.

This is how Apollo Global Management describes themselves:

At Apollo, we ask what if to create what is, because yesterday’s strategies may not solve tomorrow’s challenges. The answer? Rigorous thinking and innovative solutions for an ever-changing world.

We seek to provide flexible financing to help companies adapt, evolve and lead. We help institutions achieve long-term financial goals through investment strategies designed to deliver strong risk-adjusted returns and help individuals build lasting wealth.

We invest alongside our clients with purpose and precision, drawing on deep expertise across Credit, Equity, and Real Assets, with a sharp focus on private investment grade and ﬁxed income strategies. Our platform spans markets and geographies, bringing together deep origination capabilities and the ability to invest across market cycles—so we can meet our clients where they are.

Source: Apollo homepage.

The next diagram shows Apollo's structure.

Apollo Global believes the investment world is changing and is adjusting their portfolio based on their outlook, as shown on the following chart.

I use two measures to gauge the risk of owning a Note: interest payment coverage and Par value coverage. Looking at the Income statement, we see that the Net Income is 77X the $45MM annual payments to APOS holders.

Turning to the balance sheet, the Total Common Equity (TCE) is 36X the $600MM Par value of the Notes. When including the Minority interest, that ratio jumps to over 70X. Also note how much the TCE has climbed in 2020.

Understanding APOS

Important features include:

  • The Notes are Callable after five years but do not mature until 2053.
  • The initial coupon is 7.625%. On 9/15/2028 and every five years thereafter, the new coupon will be equal to the 5-yr TSY rate + 3.226%.
  • Current ratings are A3/BBB. Combining this with the coupon reset rule, investors are getting 3% extra yield for owning a Note with a low investment-grade rating versus a 5-yr TSY Note with high investment-grade rating.
  • The Issuer may, on one or more occasions, defer interest payments on the notes for one or more optional deferral periods of up to five consecutive years without giving rise to an Event of Default under the terms of the notes. All deferred interest must eventually be paid.
  • The Notes rank below senior debt, equal with other subordinated debt, and above all classes of stock.

Investment Analysis

Each of the following alternative choices is used to evaluate whether APOS is a good investment.

  • F&G Annuities & Life Inc. 7.950% Senior Notes due 12/15/2053 (FGN)
  • Reinsurance Group of America, Incorporated NT CAL 52 (RZC).
Factor APOS FGN RZC
Issued 8/16/23 11/30/23 9/15/22
Size (MM) 24 13.8 28
Matures 9/15/53 12/15/53 10/15/52
Call date 9/15/28 12/15/28 10/17/27
Coupon 7.625% 7.95% 7.125%
Price $25.33 $24.73 $25.35
Yield 7.50% 8.01% 7.03%
YTC 7.1% 8.3% 6.2%
YTM 7.4% 8.1% 7.0%
Float formula 5Y UST + 3.226% Fixed 5Y UST + 3.456%
  • All mature after 2052 but are all Callable by the end of 2028.
  • FGN offers the longest Call protection.
  • APOS and RZC use the 5-Yr UST rate as their base. Here, APOS offers the lower add-on.
  • The floating-rate add-on is a measure of how the market rated each issue's risk when issued (plus where overall rates were then). That is useful when comparing current yields.
  • Call risk should be judged based on how its floating coupon compares to other issues (Notes and PFDs) from the same issuer, not the others in this comparison.

Conclusion

I currently see no risk in terms of payment or bankruptcy concerning APOS as its coverage ratios are excellent, plus backstopped by Apollo Global. With its 7.5% yield, I am again giving it a Buy rating. I'm in the process of reviewing FGN but a quick look at its coverage ratios does not justify the extra yield it currently offers.

Portfolio strategy

Long-date debt should see price improvements if the entire rate curve drops as the FOMC reduces the Fed Funds Rate, or FFR. The best time to add such debt naturally is at the bottom of the rate cycle when the investor can “lock in” the best YTC and/or YTM. That said, if the rate can reset, as APOS does, guessing where rates will be at the next reset date becomes "fun." I could not find a BBB-rate forecast; what is shown is the 5-YR UST forecast, so the slope is what matters, not the level.

When picking between issues that float, I look at two components:

  • Floating rate used: The two most common are the 3-month SOFR and the 5-Yr UST. Logic says that the second normally will be higher than the first due to time difference.
  • Fixed component used: Stating the obvious, the higher the better. Looked at in another way, this would be the minimum coupon if the floating rate part drops to zero.

Closing note

As related to the above diagram showing Apollo Global's structure, I recently did an analysis of the preferred stocks offered by Athene Holdings (article link).

data.title: Gold Fields: 4% Yielder With Leverage To Gold Prices
data.description:
Gold Fields: 4% Yielder With Leverage To Gold Prices

Gold Fields Limited (GFI) is a gold miner that simply doesn’t get nearly enough love from North American investors. I can understand why—the name carries jurisdiction baggage from Ghana and South Africa that scares off a lot of the crowd chasing “safer” picks like Agnico Eagle Mines Limited (AEM) and Kinross Gold Corporation (KGC).

But when I look at the numbers today, and I mean really sit with them, this is a company producing 2.4 million gold-equivalent ounces per year, throwing off nearly $3 billion in adjusted free cash flow annually, and trading at roughly 7x forward earnings with a ~4% dividend yield. That’s a combination you almost never see in this sector, especially on the yield side of things—the mining stock yield averages just 1.88%, so you're getting a yield that's 121% greater than the average mining investor!

This is also a miner that is performing pretty well operationally. Last year, net profits surged to $3.57 billion, and the company returned $1.7 billion to its shareholders (dividends, special dividends, and buybacks).

The stock has had a monster run, but the stock has also recently pulled back more than 30% from its January highs near $60. For a buy-the-dip investor in gold miners, this is one of the more interesting setups I’ve seen lately.

Gold Fields: What Are You Buying Here?

Gold Fields owns and operates 9 gold mines in 6 countries (Australia, Ghana, Peru, Chile, and South Africa; it also owns a development project in Canada). Australia is the biggest contributor to its production, with 44% of its total output coming from three mines (Gruyere, Granny Smith, St. Ives, and Agnew).

Chile’s Salares Norte, which hit commercial production in Q3 2025 and reached steady state by year-end, now accounts for 16% of the production mix. That mine alone produced 397,000 gold-equivalent ounces in its first full year and generated $808 million in adjusted free cash flow from operations. For a mine that was a construction headache two years ago, those numbers are remarkable.

Consolidated AISC came in at $1,927 per ounce for FY2025. That’s not low by any means. I’ll come back to that, because it matters. But, I think that at a realized gold price of $3,496 per ounce, you’re looking at implied margins of roughly $1,570 per ounce across the portfolio. This company is highly leveraged to gold prices, generating close to $3 billion in adjusted free cash flow in 2025.

2026 Guidance and the Cost Question

Guidance for 2026 looks relatively strong: Gold Fields is calling for between 2.40 - 2.60 million gold equivalent ounces, which is

Gold Fields is guiding for 2.40 to 2.60 million gold-equivalent ounces in 2026, which is potentially as much as 200,000 oz AU more than in 2025. AISC guidance is $1,800 to $2,000 per ounce, with all-in costs ranging from $2,075 to $2,300.

The costs are definitely higher than I’d like to see, and it’s one of the reasons the stock trades at a discount. So, this is a company that will likely print north of $2,000 per ounce in total costs next year, well above some of its peers who produce gold at costs between $1,300-$1,800 an ounce. But I'd argue that these high costs (along with jurisdiction risk) are more than baked into the stock's valuation currently.

Inside Gold Fields' Valuation

Gold Fields is one of the cheaper gold miners (GDX) on the market currently by nearly any metric. Shares are now trading at forward price-to-earnings multiples of 6.88, a 57.45% discount to its mining peers. Furthermore, shares carry a forward EV/EBITDA of just 4.14, or 49% below peers. This is why the stock now carries an A- valuation grade on Seeking Alpha.

A forward P/E under 7x for a company producing 2.4 million ounces with $3 billion in annual free cash flow? Compare that to Agnico Eagle Mines Limited (AEM) at 13.35x forward earnings or Kinross around 9-10x. Even B2Gold Corp. (BTG), which I’ve been pounding the table on, trades around 6x forward earnings, but B2Gold is dealing with Mali and a messy Goose ramp-up, so it has its own reasons for trading at a discount. Gold Fields has nine operating mines across multiple continents. It’s not a perfect comparison, but the discount here feels overdone.

The Growth Story: 3 Million Ounces By 2030

This is where it gets interesting for longer-term holders.

Gold Fields has a strong 5-year plan that targets production growth to 3 million ounces by 2030-31, or roughly 20% growth from 2025 levels. In addition, costs should trend lower to under $2,000/oz as high-grade ounces from Windfall come into production and as its portfolio optimization starts to really pay off.

The Windfall project in Quebec is the centerpiece of that growth. I covered Osisko Mining closely for years before Gold Fields acquired it, and I’ve always liked this deposit. Average production is targeted at over 300,000 ounces per year at a steady-state AISC of roughly $1,000 per ounce. That’s significantly below the current portfolio average. Project capital is estimated at $1.7 to $1.9 billion, with a final investment decision expected in H1 2026, camp construction starting in Q2, and first gold targeted for H1 2029.

If Windfall delivers on plan, it fundamentally changes the cost profile of this company. You’re adding 300,000 high-grade ounces in a Tier 1 Canadian jurisdiction at $1,000 per ounce AISC to a portfolio that currently averages nearly double that. The blended cost structure in 2029 and beyond should look materially better than what investors are seeing today.

The Dividend Is a Sweet Bonus

Gold Fields’ new dividend policy targets a base payout of 35% of free cash flow before discretionary growth investments, with a minimum of $0.50 per share annually. Current estimates peg the dividend at $1.77 per year, which equates to a current implied yield of 4.17%.

Management has also wisely allocated $750 million to go towards special dividends and buybacks over the next 2 years. In 2025, it returned $1.7 billion to shareholders, or 54% of its free cash flow. That included $1.4 billion in base dividends, $253 million in special dividends, and $100 million in share buybacks.

At current prices, the trailing dividend yield sits around 3.7%, but the forward dividend is north of 4%. That’s meaningfully higher than most gold producers.

Agnico Eagle Mines Limited (AEM) yields under 1%. Kinross Gold Corporation (KGC) is around 1.5%. B2Gold Corp. (BTG) is under 2%. For income-focused investors who also want leveraged gold exposure, Gold Fields is one of the few names that actually pays you to wait. So if getting some yield out of your investments is important to you, then you have to take a hard look at Goldfields here.

The Risks and Why the Stock Is Cheap

I’ve spent most of this article making the bull case, so let's see what could potentially go wrong with this investment.

Ghana is a key risk factor. Gold Fields is losing the Damang mine to the government, which seized control in April 2025 after declining to renew the lease. Gold Fields will hand over the mine on April 18th of this year. So, it's losing up to 150,000 oz AU annual production from this mine. More concerning, I think, is negotiating the Tarkwa lease renewal (its other Ghana gold mine).

Tarkwa is a much bigger operation. If Ghana applies the same playbook there, it would be a serious blow. Combined, the Ghanaian operations produced 515,000 ounces last year with $568 million in adjusted free cash flow. That’s not a rounding error.

South Africa adds another layer of jurisdiction risk. South Deep produced 297,000 ounces in FY2025 and has been a chronic underperformer for Gold Fields historically, though it’s improving. Still, power instability, labor dynamics, and regulatory uncertainty in South Africa are real concerns. Between Ghana and South Africa, roughly 33% of production comes from higher-risk jurisdictions. That explains a good chunk of the valuation discount.

Cost structure. At $1,927 AISC in FY2025 and guiding to $1,800–$2,000 for 2026, Gold Fields is on the higher end among senior producers. Agnico Eagle printed $1,339 in 2025. New Gold ran around $1,075. This means Gold Fields has enormous leverage to gold on the upside, but it works both ways. A sharp gold correction would hit this stock harder than lower-cost peers.

Bottom Line

Gold Fields, at 8x forward earnings, yielding 3.7%, with a clear path to 3 million ounces by 2030 and $750 million in additional shareholder returns coming over the next two years, is a solid buy-the-dip opportunity in the gold space.

I’ll be honest: it’s not my top pick. I still like B2Gold better at these levels, where you get a cheaper valuation and a more concentrated turnaround story. And Equinox Gold Corp. (EQX) offers a similar production profile in safer jurisdictions with a cleaner cost trajectory. Similarly, Silvercorp Metals Inc. (SVM) is an excellent precious metals-focused miner to target here. But Gold Fields fills a niche that’s hard to replicate: it’s one of the only large-scale gold producers where you get genuine income alongside gold leverage. If you’re building a diversified gold portfolio and you want something that pays you while you wait for the re-rate, Gold Fields deserves a spot on the list.

data.title: SiTime: The Only Pure-Play MEMS Story In Semis, But Waiting For A Better Entry
data.description:
Introduction

SiTime's (SITM) shares have experienced an amazing run-up since last year; the stock is up more than 100% over this 1-year period, driven by investor enthusiasm over its growth prospects. Currently, SiTime's shares have experienced a slight pullback of 26% since their highs of $440 per share in early March. This is on the back of an industry-wide tech stock selloff, which was induced by the US-Iran war. You might be wondering whether this could be a potential entry point into the stock; however, I am initiating the stock with a HOLD recommendation, which is premised on a few key reasons:

First, SiTime is benefitting from AI-related growth as data centers are adopting its MEMS oscillators in both AI servers and networking equipment. The company has also secured a major design win in Apple's (AAPL) C1 internal modem, which could potentially drive large shipment growth for the company as Apple gradually replaces Qualcomm's (QCOM) modem with its own internal design. But looking at the company's expensive valuation of 60x FY2026 P/E, I prefer waiting for a further pullback in stock prices before recommending a BUY.

Business Overview

SiTime is a provider of precision timing components for the global electronics industry. Currently, most of the timing components used in the world are based on quartz resonators/oscillators, as it is a mature and low-cost technology for timekeeping. The key differentiator of SiTime is that they provide silicon-based MEMS timing components, which is a relatively new technology that is disrupting the time-keeping market. Compared to quartz, MEMS-based resonator/oscillators offer better reliability - traditional quartz resonators/oscillators are prone to vibration or shock damage, which means they are much more fragile. Besides that, quartz resonators/oscillators have higher power consumption and occupy a larger space compared to MEMS. The main drawback for MEMS, however, is its higher cost, which is why it is not widely adopted yet today.

But currently, we are at a key inflection point for the timing components - we are reaching the point where customers are getting less concerned about the cost of MEMS and more concerned about its benefits. Today, SiTime's MEMS solutions are being used in more and more applications around us, such as consumer electronics, enterprise, automotive, industrial, defence and even AI systems and data centres. SiTime has been rapidly growing over the past few years; since its IPO in 2019, the company has roughly 4x in size, from $84 million to $327 million in 2025. The future is bright for SiTime, and the company has captured less than 10% of its SAM, which indicates a long growth runway.

CED Growth Driven by AI Server Demand

The major driver for SiTime will be its communications, enterprise & data center segment (CED). The growth of this segment has greatly outpaced the growth of the overall group - in Q4 FY2025, overall revenue grew by 66%, while CED grew by 160%. The revenue mix of SiTime has shifted drastically; in the past, the company's products were mainly used by IoT/consumer devices. But now, its MEMS components are used in both server networking and AI servers as standard timing solutions.

Figure 1: SiTime's Revenue Mix Comparison

SiTime's components are used across many major areas in the AI ecosystem, such as the CPU board, ToR switches, optical transceivers, and even AECs. The primary product of SiTime is its Elite and Chorus Family, which have ASPs ranging from $3 to $12. Yes, the ASP of SiTime's products is small, which may cause you to underestimate the company. But since they are used in a wide variety of products across the data center, the sheer volume turns it into an attractive market, with a more than $1.2 billion SAM. Since the ASP of MEMS components is small relative to the AI server (which usually costs millions), there is less concern about the high cost of MEMS, as they occupy only a small portion of the server's BoM.

SiTime will be able to ride the megatrend for AI, as the company is a supplier for Nvidia servers. Heading into 2026, notable companies in the supply chain are predicting a doubling in total AI server rack shipments. As AI clusters get larger, there will be a need for both larger networking infrastructure and AI server installations, which will be beneficial to SiTime. Over the next few years, the networking industry will also be progressing to 1.6T switches, which management has guided for a double-digit increase in ASP compared to the ASP in 800G networking switches.

Figure 2: SiTime Product Application in AI Data Centers

Major Design Win in Apple's C1 Modem

Besides data centers, SiTime will benefit from the recent adoption of internal modems in iPhones by Apple. SiTime has been a long-time supplier to Apple for its non-iPhone products, such as the Apple Pencil. Apple has been trying for many years to replace Qualcomm's modems without much progress; the company struggled initially but has finally managed to develop its internally developed C1 5G modem. This modem has been adopted for Apple's iPhone 16e, which signals a turning point for SiTime's consumer/mobile business. SiTime will be able to ride on this trend, as SiTime is the exclusive MEMS oscillator supplier for Apple's internal modem.

The implications for SiTime are important, as it is set to see its iPhone-related revenue go from virtually nothing to many millions of dollars in the next few years. Analysts are expecting each modem to be paired with two SiTime MEMS oscillators, which translates to about $1 of content per device. This may again seem small, but as Apple ships more than 240 million iPhones a year, the TAM is huge for SiTime. The only question will be the adoption rate of internal modems for iPhones, as not all the 240 million shipments are equipped with modems. Currently, only Apple's iPhone 17 Air and iPhone 16e are equipped with Apple's internal modems, so the adoption rate is relatively low. But there is significant room for growth as more and more of Apple's lineup adopts this internal modem design.

Figure 3: Apple's First In-House C1 Modem

Key Acquisition of a Timing Business Competitor

Data centers and the Apple win have been large drivers of SiTime's stock price over the past year. But the most major recent development for the company will be its acquisition of Renesas's (TYO: 6723) timing business, which was only just announced in February. Although SiTime has acquired companies in the past (such as Aura), the Renesas acquisition will be the company's largest acquisition to date and will put SiTime on the path to achieving its long-term $1 billion revenue target.

The most obvious benefit from this acquisition will be in growing SiTime's CED business - 75% of revenue generated by Renesas's timing business is CED-related. Therefore, management expects to double CED revenue when the Renesas timing business is consolidated into the group. We still have a long way to go until the deal is closed, as it is only expected to happen by the end of this year. The acquisition will also boost SiTime's competitive advantage - SiTime is well-positioned in MEMS-based resonators and oscillators but lacked in terms of clock IC technology. This is exactly where Renesas's timing business excels, which is why the acquisition will greatly strengthen SiTime's competitive position.

Figure 4: SiTime Announcement of Renesas Timing Business Acquisition

Financials

The key concern about SiTime's financials post-acquisition will be the impact on its financials. First, starting with SiTime's top-line - with the incremental $300 million in revenue from Renesas, SiTime's revenue would scale to $327 million (existing revenue in 2025) + $300 million = $627 million, which would be nearly double in size post-acquisition. In terms of margins, the acquisition will be accretive to the group's gross margins; the acquired business has higher gross margins at 70% compared to SiTime's current non-GAAP gross margins of 59% for FY2025. This change will allow SiTime to move closer to its long-term margin target of 60-65%.

You might be wondering, how is SiTime going to finance this acquisition? Based on management's current plan, the company is planning to fund the acquisition with $1.5 billion in cash and 4.13 million in new shares. Management does not have enough cash on hand currently, and the cash portion would be partially funded by a $900 million debt facility, with the remainder financed by the cash on hand. The debt portion is not the primary concern, as management is aiming to reduce the group's overall leverage to under 2x within 24 months of closing the deal. The main concern to shareholders, however, would be the dilution impact, which would represent a 16% dilution based on the current 26.3 million shares outstanding.

Personally, I think that this is a great deal for SiTime, as it can practically double its business at the cost of a reasonable 16% dilution. To Renesas, the company isn't getting the short end of the stick either - the timing business is a small part of Renesas's overall revenue; by divesting its business to SiTime, SiTime will be able to pay better attention to the business and grow the synergised entity's revenue faster than Renesas could originally.

Valuation

The main issue for me currently would be SiTime's valuation. The company's fundamentals are attractive, but the stock is trading at an expensive 60x FY2026 P/E based on consensus estimates; this indicates that investors are already factoring in the structural growth of the business and have already assigned a premium valuation in return. Furthermore, the company's CED opportunity and Apple business have already been known to the market for several months and have already been baked into consensus estimates. A potential upside could be the Renesas acquisition, which I view to be beneficial and is yet to be factored into consensus numbers. But the dilution concern could cause short-term pressures on the stock, which is why I would prefer waiting on the sidelines at the moment.

Figure 5: Consensus Estimates for SiTime (FY2026) (Data by S&P Global)

SiTime is currently the only pure-play MEMS timing solutions provider listed publicly; hence, there are no direct competitors we can compare SiTime with. But there are several timing/analog component companies that we can use as a comparison for SiTime. Compared to its peers, SiTime is clearly trading at a large premium, as its peers are trading at around 27-49x FY2026 P/E. I think it is reasonable for SiTime to be trading at a more expensive level compared to the industry, as they are still small and have a longer growth runway. But the main concern putting me off is the company's absolute valuation.

Peer Analysis of SiTime and Key Metrics, Source: Quartr (Data by S&P Global)

Company Market Cap (USD) EPS (USD) P/E (2026) EPS Growth (2026)
SiTime 8,600 million 5.2 59.6 62.1%
Microchip Technology 33,600 million 1.6 37.8 19.8%
MPWR 51,700 million 21.5 48.7 21.1%
Analog Devices 150,000 million 11.4 26.8 46.7%
Texas Instrument 173,000 million 6.5 29.6 15.5%

Risk to the Company

There are several key risks that could materially affect the HOLD thesis. First, the main risk for SiTime would be a potential slowdown in AI capex. SiTime is highly reliant on the continuance of AI spending to drive the growth in its CED segment. If there is a material slowdown in hyperscaler AI capex, this could have a material negative impact on the stock. The second would be that SiTime is reliant on the growing adoption of MEMS for growth, which is a more expensive technology compared to quartz. If there is reluctance for customers to adopt MEMS over quartz resonators/oscillators, it could pose a material downside to the stock. Lastly, there is financial risk from the acquisition of the Renesas timing business, which is expected to increase the debt levels of the company. If SiTime fails to create meaningful synergies or integrate Renesas's timing business, it could pose downside risk to the stock.

Conclusion

Looking at the growth trajectory of the company, it seems that management is poised to achieve its $1 billion revenue target soon. As highlighted earlier, the company is on track to nearly double its revenue to $627 million when the Renesas acquisition closes. Factoring in the Apple growth opportunity, if Apple is able to replace 100% of its Qualcomm modems with its own internally developed modem, that alone would create an incremental $200 million in revenue for the company, which brings the total to $827 million. We have also yet to consider the organic growth from SiTime's existing CED business. Clearly, it is quite feasible for management to achieve this target. But with the current market cap of $8 billion and a forward FY2026 P/E of 60x, the market seems to already be pricing in this growth. Hence, I am initiating the stock with a 'Hold' rating and awaiting a further pullback for a potential buying opportunity.

data.title: 2 Royalty Income Streams You Don't Have To Work For
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

You've had those days when you start early, jump straight into solving problems, putting out fires, and juggling deadlines, and before you know it, it's dark outside. It was no doubt a productive day; you got things done, but you didn't really get a break. You didn't recharge. The day just disappeared. You can't even remember what you had for lunch.

That's adulthood for most of us. We stay busy, push ourselves towards career growth, and chase the next big project, the next big raise, and the next promotion. You are thirsty for more, and it matters very much, but it also means our income is tied to our time and our constant involvement. Both of these are finite.

Imagine if some part of your effort kept paying you even when you weren't actively working. Like collecting a small fee every time a piece of code you wrote was run or earning a cut each time a design you created was produced. What if the work you did once produces rewards that keep flowing?

That's the simple power behind royalties. Musicians, actors, and other artists often retain royalty rights to their content and get paid whenever their work is featured. While you can't patent most of your output at work, you can tap into the power of royalties. It's not about doing more. It's about owning streams that continue to pay, even when your day is already full.

Let's now review our top royalty picks for March.

Pick #1: KRP - Yields 9.8%

Kimbell Royalty Partners LP (KRP) holds mineral royalty interests in 17 million gross acres across 28 states, spanning key energy basins in the country. The royalty firm ended December 2025 with 85 rigs actively drilling on its acreage, and this represents 16% of all land rigs drilling in the United States. The company also declared a $0.37/share distribution for Q4, in line with our expectations, and noted that they expect 100% of the payout to be considered Return of Capital for tax purposes. This distribution will be paid on March 25th to shareholders on record on March 18, 2026.

Note: KRP is a C-corp for tax purposes and issues a 1099-DIV to shareholders.

The partnership reported a run-rate average daily production of 25,627 BOE/d, slightly above their guidance. Looking at the bigger picture, KRP has consistently grown its run-rate average daily production levels since its IPO, despite volatile commodity prices. Source

The Permian Basin continued to be the largest cash cow for KRP, representing 51% of revenues during the fourth quarter, followed by the mid-continental and Haynesville regions.

KRP uses debt and active hedging to mitigate commodity price volatility, with ~15% of production hedged on a two-year rolling basis. Speaking about debt, the company has been distributing 75% of its cash available for distribution to shareholders and retaining 25% to pay down debt. In 2025, the partnership paid down $57.4 million of its credit facility and amended and extended it through December 2030. Management noted that they did this and modestly lowered their borrowing costs by 0.35%. KRP ended 2025 with $441.5 million in debt, representing 1.5x TTM Adj. EBITDA.

During the Q4 conference call, management noted that all the partnership's acreage is leased, and advancements in E&P technology will be a strong tailwind for royalty firms, with zero CapEx on their side.

Operators that are on our properties are some of the most innovative people in America, and they're just constantly looking for ways to improve production, whether that's enhancing production techniques or drilling at different depths and different formations. And the good news is that as a mineral owner, we don't have to pay for any of that experimentation or proving out the best areas in which to apply CapEx. So it's going to be a nice windfall for us, and we believe in it. - Davis Ravnaas, CFO

KRP is a strong steward for capital return, and the company's board recently authorized a $100 million share repurchase program through December 2027. Looking ahead, management projects a daily production rate between 24,000 and 27,000 Boe/d, consistent with 2025 numbers (midpoint at 25,500 Boe/d).

The above estimations were provided by management before the military conflict in the Middle East. Global dynamics around crude oil have shifted considerably since, and we expect WTI to remain above $80, indicating strong prospects for annual distributions above $1.60/share, calculating to 9.8% yields at current share prices.

Pick #2: XOMA Preferreds - Up To 8.4% Yields

Xoma Royalty Corporation (XOMA) is a biotech royalty aggregator. In other words, they provide non-dilutive, non-debt, non-recourse funding to biotech companies towards their early-to-mid-stage clinical assets (pre-commercial and commercial stage) in exchange for economic rights to future milestones and royalty payments on eventual product revenues.

In 2017, XOMA transformed from a CapEx- and R&D-heavy biotech company into a royalty aggregator. In 2025, XOMA made 22 portfolio additions, ending the year with 7 commercial and 14 phase-3 assets. It is important to note that XOMA earns not only from royalties on commercial product sales but also from milestone payments triggered as an asset progresses through clinical development or reaches commercial achievements. Royalties are often structured to be in the low to mid single digits. Take, for example, XOMA's 0.5% royalty interest in Vabysmo (Roche), an intravitreal injection used to treat renal diseases like age-related macular degeneration, diabetic macular edema, and retinal vein occlusion. The royalty interest % may seem small, but peak sales for this drug are expected to be over $8 billion, indicating that XOMA stands to collect up to $40 million annually from this drug. Source

XOMA generated positive cash flow from operations in 2025, with over $50 million in portfolio receipts, with two-thirds from royalty payments and the rest from milestone payments. The chart below demonstrates the company's trajectory for cash generation.

There are at least three major data announcements expected in 2026, implying more milestone payments ahead. XOMA ended 2025 with over 120 assets (up from 60 at the end of 2023) and with $83 million in cash on hand.

During 2025, XOMA made $16 million in share repurchases while spending $5.4 million on preferred dividends. The company doesn't pay any dividends on its common stock but has two perpetual preferreds.

  • 8.625% Series A, Cumulative Perpetual Preferred Stock (XOMAP) - Yield 8.2%

  • 8.375% Series B, Cumulative Perpetual Preferred Stock (XOMAO) - Yield 8.4%

XOMA preferreds pay qualified dividends and trade post their call dates (for over four years). Despite their call risk and modest premium to par pricing, these preferreds present attractive income potential at current prices, supported by a unique, rapidly expanding royalty-aggregation model that is poised to generate substantial future cash flows.

Conclusion

At High Dividend Opportunities, we insist that income should come from multiple, reliable sources and not depend on how many hours you can work. The goal is to build a portfolio that sends you paychecks whether you're tackling a busy day or taking a well-deserved vacation with family. We buy investments to create royalties for participating in the financial markets. Ongoing market activity generates reliable income without demanding our constant time and effort.

Your time is finite, but your income doesn't have to be. When your portfolio is filled with durable, royalty-like cash streams, you stop trading your time for paychecks and start collecting them while living your life. And before long, you reach the point where you can honestly say, "Boy, these dividends are making me thirsty."

data.title: Why Future S&P 500 Returns May Disappoint - And How I'm Positioning Now
data.description:
Introduction

We often discuss inflation, as it’s somewhat of a silent killer of wealth. While a $100 bill will still be a $100 bill a few years from now, it tends to buy less over time. Since the early 2000s, the purchasing power of the dollar has lost roughly half of its value. Personally, I believe that number is higher, but using official numbers (see below), it’s roughly 50%.

Now, don’t get me wrong. This doesn’t mean life gets worse over time. $1,000 in the early 1910s got you goods worth less than $80 in the 1990s. However, I think we can all agree that life in 1990s America was better than in the 1910s.

As negative as inflation may be, consistent wage hikes and innovation tend to improve life over time. However, there are certain periods when inflation is an issue. Currently, for example, I think it’s fair to say that younger people are in a tricky spot due to factors like high home prices, elevated mortgage rates, and AI disruption in many white-collar jobs.

Furthermore, tough times can result in serious wealth challenges.

That’s what this article is about, as I think it’s one of the most serious articles I have written this year so far.

So, without further ado, let’s keep this intro short and get right to it!

We Are So Spoiled. Will It Last?

I started investing in 2011. While that is nothing compared to the track record that some of you may have, there are many on Wall Street who joined after me. And in general, younger generations have seen some of the best years on Wall Street. Since 2011, for example, we have been through two bear markets, one in 2020 and one in 2022.

I’m convinced that if I had started making good money 1-2 years earlier, I would have achieved all of my financial targets before turning 30. But it is what it is. While I cannot complain in my situation, we need to play the cards we’re dealt.

That brings me to the most important chart of this article. Below, you see the inflation-adjusted S&P 500. It’s simply the ratio between the S&P 500 and CPI inflation.

It’s a nice chart that tells us that any long-term investor would have made a lot of money over time by simply doing nothing, except for consistently buying the index.

That’s the good news.

The bad news is that, over time, the market has been through prolonged periods without any real gains (real means adjusted for inflation). For example, between the mid-1960s and the early 1990s (that’s roughly 25 years), the S&P 500 had no positive real return.

Between the early 2000s and 2014, it happened again. Investors saw 14 years without a net gain. Moreover, if you had bought in 1955, the market would have tested your initial entry again in 1982. There are many such instances that tell us that the uptrend we have seen since 2009 (with some bumps along the way) isn’t guaranteed.

And that’s my point.

I’m 30 now. If we were to enter a period of >20 years without net gains (this is NOT a prediction), I would be 50+ by the time I enjoy real capital gains again. Between then and now, most of my wealth would come from new capital additions without the post-inflation capital appreciation tailwind.

Again, this isn’t a prediction. I’m just painting a dark picture here based on past events.

Why Risks Are Elevated

Everything I wrote so far, I could have written in any market, as I simply observed and gave you some facts from the past. What matters right now is that risks are rising.

For example, during the 1970s, which was in the middle of a horrible return period, as we just discussed, the market was quite expensive, although way less expensive than it is right now. That difference is mainly caused by factors like higher margins.

This ruined the party for investors. While earnings continued to grow (see the chart below), the stock market didn’t rise, as its valuation multiple went down. That more than offset earnings growth. Note that the chart below is from 2016, but for the purpose of this article, it works just fine, as we perfectly see the components of the market’s total return.

Essentially, this explains why, over time, a “high” P/E multiple indicates subdued longer-term returns, as the odds are rising that positive earnings growth is offset by multiple compression.

Think about it like this. If Company XYZ generates $10 in EPS and trades at 30x earnings, it has a $300 stock price. Now, it grows that EPS number to $12, yet the market believes it’s only worth 20x due to AI disruption (or any other factor). Now, it trades at $240 despite positive earnings growth. Adobe Inc. (ADBE) investors have found this out the hard way recently.

To make matters worse, inflation further pressured the market’s real return.

Currently, inflation risks are elevated. For example, 1-year inflation swaps are back at 3.2%, while breakeven rates, which are way more volatile, are above 5.0%. The real number will likely be somewhere in the middle if crude oil prices remain high and disruption in other areas like agriculture, chemicals, and natural gas keeps global prices at a high level.

Moreover, I see structural risks. As I have often written before, I believe in a “higher for longer” scenario until we get the full deflationary effect from AI. This inflation is fueled by factors like energy bottlenecks, deglobalization, and government policies. That’s the “run it hot” thesis, which is simply based on a likely path where the government will accept higher inflation for the sake of broadening economic growth and lowering the real value of the national debt.

If that plan works out, it could be great for the economy. Yet inflation is a major risk.

Here are some thoughts on this from Main Street Alpha, an upcoming Investing Group service on Seeking Alpha I'm involved with that is launching soon:

Therefore, the monetary and fiscal authorities are quietly aligned in letting inflation run hot. It is a stealth default. They are inflating away the debt burden while publicly discussing the rising cost of living for obvious political purposes. This creates an environment where running it hot using inflation becomes an unofficial policy.

We are transitioning from a globalist, low-inflation environment, where tech multiples could expand to the moon on the back of zero-interest-rate policy, to a nationalist, high-nominal-growth era. - Main Street Alpha

Moreover, this is what we wrote regarding the market rotation, which is entirely fueled by this economic transition, so to speak:

[...] they are rotating aggressively into "deep value" plays. The logic here is incredibly simple, yet widely misunderstood by retail investors: if the regime is shifting toward nominal growth and persistent inflation, the biggest winners won't be the innovators. The biggest winners will be the indebted, the asset-heavy, and the monopolistic. - Main Street Alpha

That would be an issue, as the market is massively overweight in tech stocks.

Generally speaking, when the market is heavily concentrated, it tends to risk subdued returns, as any rotation would mean that the “rest of the market” would have to do the heavy lifting. Regardless of the type of rotation we’re talking about, this tends to hurt future returns.

In the 1960s and 1970s, that was the case as well. Furthermore, back then, inflation was a risk.

Today’s market is very different from the 1960s and 1970s, yet there are key macro similarities.

Now What?

As I said, I wish I were a few years older (all else being equal) so I could have started my career a bit earlier. But it is what it is. I’m not bearish on the S&P 500 in the sense that I expect the index to drop, but I am convinced that the returns of the next few years will not be as rosy as in the past decade.

In fact, I think the odds are against us, meaning we could see a prolonged period of subdued returns.

Does that mean I’m worried? No. Absolutely not.

There are plenty of opportunities if we simply look under the market’s hood or in other areas. That’s the good thing about a top-heavy market, as there tends to be great value behind the stocks that get all the attention and are in the market’s driver's seat.

Gold is one of them. In the 1970s, it was one of the best assets to own. While one factor is that it became more accessible, it also benefited from inflation and subdued equity returns.

I own no gold and no miners, yet I hope to change that this year.

Presently, gold is weak, which may be surprising in light of inflation and war risks. However, this is what we call the “scarcity plot.” Basically, because of the war, the U.S. is dominating supply chains like energy, agriculture, and capital. It’s becoming a bit of an oasis in the global macro landscape. That’s bullish for the dollar and temporarily bad for gold.

I’m paraphrasing and cutting it short, but that’s basically the gist of it.

It’s also why we are looking to buy gold (miners) on weakness for the next few years.

Moreover, I stick to my (cyclical) value thesis.

As I wrote in an article titled “Forget FANG - I’m Betting On ‘TOLL’ Stocks Instead.”

I explained why I believe hard asset stocks will drive the market going forward, mainly due to their ability to offset inflation risks, benefit from broadening growth, and, in some cases, be at the receiving end of trillions in long-term AI spending.

TOLL stands for:

  • Tangible assets (companies that are hard to disrupt by AI).
  • Oligopoly (limited competition).
  • Low incremental capital intensity (the ability to scale margins based on past CapEx investments). Think of a railroad that has all tracks in place and can now grow margins.
  • Long-duration cash flows (somewhat predictable long-term cash flows).

This includes industrial stocks. I think the State Street Industrial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLI) and many of its holdings will beat the S&P 500 for many years to come.

The same goes for basic materials, energy, and real estate, just to name a few.

And even in the tech space, I think some will do just fine. One of my favorites is Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), which I will give a lot more attention going forward, as I promised.

Moreover, while I don’t have the exact data to back it up, knowing who my reader base is, I think most of us who own quality dividend (growth) stocks, cyclical value like the ones I just mentioned, and precious metals should be in a good spot to generate “decent” longer-term returns.

The biggest risk, in my view, lies in being overly dependent on the S&P 500 or Big Tech/software. I may be wrong, but unless inflation comes crashing down and the growth rebound remains a thesis, we’ll likely end up seeing subdued S&P 500 returns for a prolonged period.

Takeaway

I don’t write this stuff to scare anyone. Our time is way too valuable for that.

Articles like this one are to prepare investors and explain the shifts in risk/reward I see for major assets, in this case, the S&P 500 and some of its biggest holdings.

The way I see it is that inflation is likely to become a bigger headwind for purchasing power and market valuations, which, historically speaking, is not a great sign for what’s to come.

That’s why I am advocating for a bigger rotation into hard assets, TOLL-like stocks, high-quality dividend growers, and assets like gold. If you own any of these, you’ll likely be fine.

I think the biggest risk is the consensus of the past few years.

data.description:
Our philosophy

Simply put, the upward inflection in stock prices can be a confirmatory signal of fundamental improvement. Our approach to investing is based on our view that fundamentals ultimately drive stock prices. Through deep fundamental research, we aim to exploit market anomalies to identify stocks that should outperform the market. Once we identify an opportunity, the signal of upward inflection in price provides the impetus to invest. We believe that investing in companies that demonstrate an ability to compound capital over time, maintain strong fundamental attributes and trade at discounts to intrinsic value will lead to durable outperformance over a full market cycle. Our work relies on proprietary research and our experience investing over many different market cycles. We believe that the integrity and intensity of our research process leads to success. Our clients benefit from in-depth internally generated research from Columbia Threadneedle Investments' outstanding fundamental analyst team, quantitative research department and big-data team. Our passion for investing and deep belief in our philosophy are foundational to our strategy, which we are proud to offer our clients. This philosophy has been employed on behalf of our clients for years, and our north star (our philosophy) will continue to guide us forward for years to come.
